data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was driven by a systemic fragility in the palatial redistribution economy where hyper-specialization created a single point of failure; when seismic activity or minor trade disruptions broke the supply of critical bronze ingredients, the entire socio-political structure unraveled because local communities lacked the autonomy to survive independently.", "mechanism": "Cascading systemic failure triggered by supply chain interruption in a non-resilient, centralized network.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Cyprus", "independent rural villages"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeological evidence showing robust local subsistence economies continuing uninterrupted within palace zones during the transition period.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "This candidate leans on the 'complexity collapse' theory, emphasizing internal structural weakness over external shock."}
